---
title: "UIAO Modernization Master Plan — Phase 0 Planning Document"
subtitle: "Program scope, architectural planes, and foundational dependencies"
doc-type: master-plan
phase: "Phase 0 — Planning"
audience: "CIO · CISO · ISSO · Authorizing Official (AO) · 3PAO"
canon-source: "src/uiao/canon/modernization-registry.yaml"
derived-from: "uiao canon"
aspirational: true
date: 2026-04-24
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"
---

# UIAO Modernization Master Plan — Phase 0 Planning Document {.unnumbered}

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
## Series Map — you are reading **document 1 of 5**
[Series index](index.qmd) ·
**Phase 0 Planning** (this document) →
[Phase 1 Architecture](phase-1-architecture.qmd) →
[Boundary Modernization Appendix](boundary-modernization-appendix.qmd) →
[Compliance & ATO Alignment](compliance-and-ato-alignment.qmd) →
[90-Day Execution Plan](90-day-execution-plan.qmd)
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Document Purpose
This document establishes the Phase 0 planning baseline for the Unified
Identity–Addressing–Overlay Architecture (UIAO) Modernization Program. It
defines program scope, the architectural planes in scope for modernization,
the foundational dependencies that govern sequencing, and the federal
mandates the program aligns to. Subsequent phase documents (Phase 1
Architecture, Boundary Modernization Appendix, Compliance & ATO Alignment,
and the 90-Day Execution Plan) derive from this baseline.
:::

---

## 1. Executive Summary

The Unified Identity–Addressing–Overlay Architecture (UIAO) is the canonical
substrate that binds identity, device, session, routing, security, evidence,
and compliance signals into a single governed fabric. Its purpose is to
deliver a deterministic, provenance-tracked, drift-resistant foundation for
federal enterprise modernization — one in which every control outcome can be
traced to a canonical source, every boundary decision is observable, and
every change is attributable.

**Modernization goals.** The program pursues four outcomes in parallel:
stabilize identity across hybrid directory estates; restore end-to-end
telemetry and signal visibility across cloud, WAN, and endpoint paths;
enable Zero Trust enforcement anchored to canonical identity and device
state; and establish continuous Authority to Operate (cATO) by making
compliance evidence a by-product of normal operations rather than an
after-the-fact collection exercise.

**The Boundary Gap as a structural dependency.** A complete modernization of
the UIAO planes is gated by a structural constraint: the current
authorization boundary does not preserve a coherent set of identity,
location, session, telemetry, media, and policy signals as traffic
traverses cloud, WAN, and perimeter boundaries. This condition — referred
to throughout this document as the *Boundary Gap* — is architectural, not
organizational. It is treated as a modernization prerequisite because every
downstream plane (Security, Evidence, Compliance, Continuous ATO) depends
on the signals the Boundary Gap currently attenuates.

**Federal alignment.** The program is framed as direct alignment with
existing federal mandates and guidance, not a net-new initiative:

| Mandate / Framework | Alignment |
|---|---|
| **Executive Order 14028** (Improving the Nation's Cybersecurity) | Zero Trust transition, endpoint detection, logging maturity, and supply-chain integrity |
| **CISA SCuBA** (Secure Cloud Business Applications) | Baseline configuration enforcement and drift detection for M365 and cloud services |
| **FedRAMP Rev 5** | Control tailoring, continuous monitoring, and evidence chain for boundary-spanning services |
| **OMB M-22-09** | Federal Zero Trust architecture strategy, identity and device conditions of access |
| **NIST SP 800-207** | Zero Trust architecture reference model |

Phase 0 produces a common frame of reference for program stakeholders. It
does not commit scope to any single vendor, adapter, or solution pattern.

---

## 2. Modernization Scope

The program modernizes the full set of UIAO planes and the supporting
boundary systems they depend on. Each plane is treated as a first-class
unit of work with its own signals, controls, and evidence surface.

### 2.1 Planes in Scope

| Plane | Scope Summary | Primary Signals |
|---|---|---|
| **Identity Plane** | Directory modernization (AD → Entra ID), org hierarchy, dynamic groups, administrative units, delegation, lifecycle automation, privileged access | User, group, role, AU, authentication, MFA, PIM |
| **Device Plane** | Endpoint posture, management state (Intune / equivalent), compliance policy, attestation, patch state | Compliance status, configuration profile, attestation, posture telemetry |
| **Server Plane** | Server identity and posture, workload identity, service principal governance, legacy server retirement pathway | Server OrgPath, workload identity, host telemetry |
| **Routing Plane** | WAN / SD-WAN / SASE routing posture, traffic steering, egress policy, regional routing determinism | Route state, path selection, egress identity, regional drift indicators |
| **Security Plane** | Conditional Access, Zero Trust enforcement, threat analytics integration, containment primitives | Access decision, risk signal, detection, response action |
| **Evidence Plane** | Provenance graph, signal capture, OSCAL artifact generation, immutable evidence trail | Provenance records, OSCAL components, SAR / SSP artifacts |
| **Compliance Plane** | Control catalog mapping, KSI alignment, POA&M management, assessment artifact generation | Control state, assessment finding, KSI outcome |
| **Drift Detection** | Cross-plane drift scanning (schema, value, hierarchy, orphan, phantom), remediation workflow | Drift category, drift severity, remediation provenance |
| **Continuous ATO** | Ongoing authorization posture, monitoring cadence, automated evidence refresh, AO decision support | Authorization state, monitoring freshness, re-assessment trigger |
| **Boundary Modernization** | Authorization boundary redesign to preserve identity, location, session, telemetry, media, and policy signals end-to-end | Boundary crossings, signal continuity, policy enforcement points |

### 2.2 Out of Scope for Phase 0

- Vendor selection and adapter-level procurement decisions.
- Mission-system-specific data migration plans.
- Per-office tactical cutover sequencing (addressed in the 90-Day Execution Plan).
- Any activity that requires a ratified Phase 1 Architecture Document as input.

---

## 3. Problem Statement

Federal modernization programs consistently encounter three classes of
challenge that UIAO is designed to address. This section describes those
challenges in architectural, not organizational, terms.

**Signal loss across boundaries.** As traffic and identity assertions
traverse the authorization boundary — from endpoint to cloud, from on-
premises directory to cloud identity provider, from regional WAN to
centralized egress — signals that are essential for access decisions,
detection, and audit are attenuated, transformed, or dropped. The effect
is a control surface that appears complete on paper but cannot be
continuously evidenced end-to-end.

**Visibility gaps in the operational plane.** Telemetry is typically
collected per system rather than per journey. When a single user action
spans identity, device, routing, and application planes, there is no
canonical way to reconstruct the full chain. This creates blind spots for
both security operations and compliance assessment.

**Architectural constraints from legacy substrates.** Active Directory,
legacy WAN, and perimeter-centric security models encoded governance
relationships implicitly — through OU structure, VLAN topology, and
firewall zones. Modern cloud substrates do not preserve those implicit
relationships. Without an explicit governance fabric, migration produces
flat directories, ungoverned group sprawl, lost delegation boundaries, and
infrastructure services that no longer have an authoritative anchor.

These challenges are systemic and architectural. They do not trace to any
single team, vendor, or prior decision, and this document does not
attribute them to one.

---

## 4. FedRAMP Boundary Gap (Architectural Dependency)

The Boundary Gap is a structural dependency that must be addressed as a
modernization prerequisite. It is introduced here in the terms of the
signals it affects, the cloud behaviors it changes, and the compliance
implications that follow.

### 4.1 Missing or Attenuated Signals

At the current authorization boundary, six signal classes are not
preserved end-to-end:

| Signal Class | Definition | Consequence when Attenuated |
|---|---|---|
| **Identity** | Authenticated user, role, group, AU, privileged state | Access decisions cannot bind to canonical identity across boundary crossings |
| **Location** | Originating network context, region, country, trust posture of the originating segment | Conditional Access and regional compliance rules degrade to approximations |
| **Session** | Continuity of session from initial authentication through downstream service calls | Downstream services cannot enforce session-level revocation or risk re-evaluation |
| **Telemetry** | Per-journey observability across identity, device, routing, and application planes | Incident reconstruction and continuous monitoring become partial |
| **Media** | Voice, video, and collaboration traffic classification and routing posture | Real-time workload policy cannot be evidenced at the boundary |
| **Policy** | The set of enforcement decisions applied at each hop, with attribution | Policy enforcement points cannot be audited as a single control fabric |

### 4.2 Impact on Cloud Behavior

When these signals are not preserved, cloud services exhibit three
recurring patterns:

1. **Identity flattening.** Cloud identity stores receive a subset of the
   original governance context; downstream enforcement must reconstruct
   what was lost, often heuristically.
2. **Regional drift.** Services that are expected to behave identically
   across regions diverge in observable state because boundary signals
   vary by path.
3. **Evidence fragmentation.** Compliance evidence is assembled from
   multiple systems whose signal sets do not reconcile, producing gaps
   that must be explained narratively rather than shown evidentially.

### 4.3 Compliance Implications

The Boundary Gap has direct implications for FedRAMP Rev 5 continuous
monitoring, SCuBA baseline enforcement, and the cATO model:

- **Continuous monitoring** presumes a stable, reproducible evidence surface.
  Attenuated signals produce monitoring cadence that is valid point-in-time
  but cannot be independently verified over a window.
- **SCuBA baseline conformance** relies on the ability to observe policy
  enforcement at the boundary. Missing policy signals make conformance
  assessable only by proxy.
- **cATO** requires a monotonically improving evidence chain. Without
  boundary signal continuity, the chain resets at each boundary crossing,
  which inhibits the ongoing authorization posture AOs are asked to sustain.

### 4.4 Framing as a Modernization Prerequisite

Because every downstream plane — Security, Evidence, Compliance, and
Continuous ATO — depends on signals that the Boundary Gap currently
attenuates, the program treats Boundary Modernization as a prerequisite
work stream rather than an adjacent one. Phase 1 Architecture will encode
this sequencing; Phase 0 simply establishes it as a design constraint.

---

## 5. Objectives & Outcomes

The program's objectives are expressed as outcomes that can be observed
and evidenced, not activities to be performed.

| # | Objective | Observable Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stabilize identity** | A single authoritative identity state per principal, with provenance, across hybrid directory estates |
| 2 | **Restore visibility** | End-to-end journey telemetry reconstructable across identity, device, routing, and application planes |
| 3 | **Enable Zero Trust** | Access decisions bound to canonical identity, device posture, and session risk at every enforcement point, consistent with OMB M-22-09 and NIST SP 800-207 |
| 4 | **Enable SCuBA alignment** | Continuous, evidenced conformance with CISA SCuBA baselines for in-scope cloud services, with drift detection and remediation provenance |
| 5 | **Enable continuous ATO** | Ongoing authorization posture sustained by automated evidence refresh, with AO decision support grounded in canonical artifacts |
| 6 | **Improve user experience** | Consistent access experience across headquarters and field offices, with deterministic routing and identity resolution regardless of originating location |

Each objective has a corresponding set of leading and lagging indicators,
which will be formalized in the Phase 1 Architecture Document.

---

## 6. Deliverables

Phase 0 commits the program to the following deliverable set. Each
deliverable is a governed artifact with a defined owner, a canonical
source location, and a downstream consumer.

| Deliverable | Summary |
|---|---|
| **UIAO Architecture** | Authoritative description of planes, signals, interfaces, and control bindings across the full UIAO substrate |
| **Boundary Modernization Plan** | Redesign plan for the authorization boundary that preserves the six signal classes end-to-end and encodes the Boundary Gap as a sequencing dependency |
| **Compliance & ATO Plan** | FedRAMP Rev 5 control tailoring, KSI alignment, SCuBA conformance strategy, and the continuous ATO operating model |
| **Signal Restoration Framework** | The normative framework for capturing, carrying, and evidencing the six signal classes across boundary crossings |
| **90-Day Execution Timeline** | Sequenced execution plan covering the first ninety days of work, scoped to activities enabled by the preceding deliverables |

Each deliverable is defined further in a companion document during
Phase 1; Phase 0 records only the deliverable commitment and the
inter-deliverable dependencies.

---

## 7. Risks & Dependencies

The program operates under six foundational risks and dependencies, each
of which is to be tracked continuously and revisited at every phase gate.

| # | Risk / Dependency | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Boundary signal constraints** | Until boundary modernization is complete, downstream planes operate with attenuated signals, producing partial evidence surfaces |
| 2 | **Legacy WAN routing** | Existing WAN topology may not support deterministic regional routing required for consistent access experience and policy enforcement |
| 3 | **Telemetry limitations** | Per-system telemetry collection may not yet support per-journey reconstruction; instrumentation gaps must be closed before visibility objectives can be evidenced |
| 4 | **Region drift** | Regional divergence in cloud and network state can produce inconsistent enforcement and compliance posture; drift detection must be in place early |
| 5 | **Identity continuity** | Hybrid directory states risk producing duplicate, orphan, or phantom principals during transition; identity continuity is a precondition for every downstream plane |
| 6 | **Compliance evidence gaps** | Evidence that depends on boundary-spanning signals will be incomplete until the Boundary Gap is closed; the Compliance & ATO Plan must explicitly account for this sequencing |

Risks 1 through 6 are treated as design constraints, not contingencies.
They shape the sequencing of deliverables rather than being managed as
probabilistic events.

---

## 8. Next Steps

Phase 0 closes when this document is ratified by the program's governance
body. The following artifacts are initiated in sequence, with explicit
dependencies on the Phase 0 baseline:

1. **Prepare Phase 1 Architecture Document.** Formalizes the UIAO planes,
   their interfaces, and the signal model. Consumes Sections 2, 4, and 5
   of this document as normative input.
2. **Prepare Boundary Modernization Appendix.** Expands Section 4 into a
   concrete redesign plan for the authorization boundary, including the
   Signal Restoration Framework and the sequencing constraints it imposes
   on downstream planes.
3. **Prepare Compliance & ATO Alignment.** Maps UIAO planes to FedRAMP
   Rev 5 controls, CISA SCuBA baselines, and KSI outcomes, and defines
   the continuous ATO operating model.
4. **Prepare 90-Day Execution Plan.** Sequences the first ninety days of
   execution against the ratified Phase 1 Architecture, Boundary
   Modernization Appendix, and Compliance & ATO Alignment.

No execution activity begins until the corresponding upstream artifact is
ratified. This sequencing is intentional: it ensures that every downstream
commitment traces to a governed Phase 0 baseline and that the Boundary
Gap is treated as the structural dependency it is.

---

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
## Document Governance
This Phase 0 Planning Document is a canonical program artifact. Changes
are governed by the canon-change protocol; substantive revisions require
an ADR, a schema check, and a drift scan. Derived consumers (Phase 1
Architecture, Boundary Modernization Appendix, Compliance & ATO
Alignment, 90-Day Execution Plan) cite this document as their upstream
source.
:::
